POLARIS must hit SOTA on RACE (DeepResearch Bench, github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench).
RACE = reference-based LLM judge (openai/gpt-5.5), 4 DIMENSIONS with per-task dynamic weights:
Comprehensiveness, Insight/Depth, Instruction-Following, Readability. Frontier systems (ChatGPT/Gemini
deep research, FS-Researcher arXiv 2602.01566) score ~0.45-0.50. POLARIS's FIRST real number:
DRB task72 overall = 0.2685 (faithfulness PASS) — WELL BELOW SOTA. That report was composed by the
OUTLINE wheel's OWN compose, NOT the improved compose_fix (16-way concurrency). Proof artifacts +
scorer live in /home/polaris/wt/outline_agent (outputs/, scripts/score_report_race.py); the agentic
corpus is data/cp4_corpus_s3gear_329.json (329 baskets, 'Generative AI's Impact on Employment').
Best compose = /home/polaris/wt/compose_fix (sharded, judge/writer 16-way, glm-5.2 max tokens).

HONESTY: report the REAL per-dimension numbers. If a dimension is genuinely weak, say so. Do NOT tune
the judge to inflate the score. The goal is a TRUE climb toward 0.48, not a flattering number.
Source the OpenRouter key: set -a; . /workspace/POLARIS/.env; set +a  (never print it). Watch cost —
score EXISTING renders where possible; a full new render only when needed. If 429/hang, say so.


Get the TRUE current number, two ways:
(1) MULTI-TASK: one task is noise. Score POLARIS on 2-3 MORE DRB tasks (pick tasks whose topic the
329-basket AI corpus can actually answer, or the nearest reference tasks the harness supports) with
score_report_race.py, same gpt-5.5 judge, to get a MEAN, not a single point.
(2) BEST-COMPOSE: the 0.2685 used the outline's own compose. If feasible without a huge spend, render
the SAME task72 outline through the best compose (compose_fix, 16-way) and re-score — does the best
compose lift the number? If a full re-render is too costly right now, say so and skip, don't fake it.
Report the scores, the mean, and whether best-compose beats outline-own-compose. Diagnosis context:
{
  "dimensions": {
    "comprehensiveness": {
      "score": 0.2915,
      "weight": 0.29,
      "contribution": 0.0845,
      "lost_points": 0.2055,
      "ref_multiple": "2.43x"
    },
    "insight": {
      "score": 0.2516,
      "weight": 0.32,
      "contribution": 0.0805,
      "lost_points": 0.2395,
      "ref_multiple": "2.97x"
    },
    "instruction_following": {
      "score": 0.2527,
      "weight": 0.25,
      "contribution": 0.0632,
      "lost_points": 0.1868,
      "ref_multiple": "2.96x"
    },
    "readability": {
      "score": 0.2839,
      "weight": 0.14,
      "contribution": 0.0397,
      "lost_points": 0.1003,
      "ref_multiple": "2.52x"
    }
  },
  "weakest": "insight",
  "why": "The reference report (task 72, /home/polaris/wt/outline_agent/third_party/deep_research_bench/data/test_data/cleaned_data/reference.jsonl) is a 9,029-word / 33-heading / 8-section literature review with TWO summary tables; POLARIS (/home/polaris/wt/outline_agent/third_party/deep_research_bench/data/test_data/raw_data/polaris_agentic_run1.jsonl, the OUTLINE-wheel compose) is 1,655 words / 8 headings / ZERO tables = 18% of reference length. RACE normalizes each dimension as target/(target+reference), so POLARIS's 0.25-0.29 means the reference out-scored it ~2.4-3.0x on every axis. Concretely, four things the reference does that POLARIS does not: (1) INSIGHT (weakest, 2.97x behind): the reference builds explicit theoretical scaffolding (task-based displacement/reinstatement/productivity effects a la Acemoglu-Restrepo, SBTC vs RBTC/routine-bias, substitute/complement/augment) and after every section adds a stepped-back analytical synthesis paragraph ('This implies...', 'the direction of AI innovation itself is a key determinant...'), plus a whole section on the productivity-paradox / J-curve and dedicated 'areas of disagreement' + 'research gaps' sections. POLARIS is a data-dump: disconnected fact sentences each ending in a bracket citation, no cross-study synthesis, no discussion of contradictions/heterogeneity, no 'so what'. (2) COMPREHENSIVENESS (2.43x behind): reference covers theory -> empirics (robotics, AI-specific, meta-analyses, firm-level) -> sectoral disruptions (manufacturing/logistics, healthcare/finance/transport, creative/marketing/service) -> wages/inequality/skills -> future outlook -> policy (education, safety nets, taxation, regulation) -> conclusion. POLARIS has 5 thin sections mislabeled with CLINICAL-TRIAL template headers ('Efficacy', 'Safety', 'Comparative', 'Long-term Outcomes') and no intro, no policy section, no conclusion. (3) INSTRUCTION-FOLLOWING (2.96x behind): task 72 demands 'only high-quality, English-language JOURNAL articles', yet POLARIS cites MIT Sloan ideas blog [2], a Wharton budget-model blog [17], articleoneadvisors.com [9], abacademies [13], and OECD/ILO/IMF working papers/reports (many tier UNKNOWN/T3/T4) - the reference's Table 1 cites named journals (JPE, REStat, JLE, Res Policy, IJM). POLARIS also violates the prompt's mandated summary table (the embedded prompt even specified exact column headers; it produced none). Worse, POLARIS was composed against a DIFFERENT query (its own title/prompt is 'Generative AI's impact on employment, research before June 2023, positive/negative/challenges/opportunities') than the RACE task-72 prompt it is scored against, and it ends with a 'Methods'/'Limitations' block dumping its OWN pipeline internals (corpus filename cp4_corpus_s3gear_329.json, tier distribution T1=4%, telemetry 'contradictions_detected', 'not_comparable_pairings', glm-5.2 generator) - meta-commentary about the tool that a literature review must never contain. (4) READABILITY (2.52x behind, POLARIS's least-bad dim): reference uses clean numbered hierarchy, topic sentences, bulleted mechanisms and tables; POLARIS uses run-on citation-stuffed paragraphs under wrong headers.",
  "overall": "Verified real numbers from /home/polaris/wt/outline_agent/third_party/deep_research_bench/results/race/polaris_agentic_run1/ (race_result.txt + raw_results.jsonl); dimension weights from data/criteria_data/criteria.jsonl task-72 'dimension_weight' = {comprehensiveness 0.29, insight 0.32, instruction_following 0.25, readability 0.14}. Overall = sum(weight*score) = 0.268 (matches reported 0.2685). Ranked by lost points (weight*(1-score)): insight 0.2395 > comprehensiveness 0.2055 > instruction_following 0.1868 > readability 0.1003. Insight is weakest because it carries BOTH the highest weight (0.32) AND the lowest score (0.2516) - the reference scored ~3x POLARIS there. NOTE: this 0.2685 is the OUTLINE wheel's OWN compose (polaris_agentic_run1, 12,985 chars), NOT the improved compose_fix; a like-for-like re-score of a compose_fix render on task 72 would be the honest next measurement. No new render was run (cost/honesty); this is analysis of the existing scored artifact.",
  "quick_wins": [
    "Kill the clinical-template outline (Efficacy/Safety/Comparative/Long-term Outcomes) and use topic-shaped sections: Intro+4IR framing, Theoretical frameworks, Empirical findings, Sectoral disruptions, Wages/inequality/skills, Policy, Conclusion+research gaps - directly lifts comprehensiveness and instruction-following",
    "Add 1-2 structured summary tables (study x geography x methodology x employment/wage findings; and policy recommendations) - reference has two, POLARIS has zero, and task-72's embedded prompt explicitly mandated a table",
    "Strip the pipeline-internals 'Methods'/'Limitations' block (corpus filename, tier %, telemetry fields, glm-5.2) - meta-commentary about the tool tanks readability and instruction-following",
    "Enforce journal-only source filtering for this task: drop MIT Sloan/Wharton/advisor blogs and org reports (tier UNKNOWN/T3/T4), cite named peer-reviewed journals - task-72 constraint is 'only high-quality English-language journal articles'",
    "Raise insight: after each evidence cluster, add a synthesis paragraph naming the mechanism, reconciling contradictory findings (e.g. meta-analytic near-zero-but-heterogeneous wage effects), and stating implications - not just citation-stuffed fact lists",
    "Expand length ~4-5x (target ~7-9k words) so coverage can physically approach the 9,029-word reference; current 1,655 words caps comprehensiveness",
    "Re-run the score against the EXACT task-72 prompt with a report actually composed for that prompt (restructuring impact of AI, 4IR framing) - the scored render answered a different embedded query, which structurally caps instruction-following"
  ]
}

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Score a composed POLARIS report with RACE (DeepResearch Bench) against a benchmark task.
3	
4	RACE (third_party/deep_research_bench/deepresearch_bench_race.py) is REFERENCE-BASED: it matches
5	the target article, the reference article, and the per-task criteria by EXACT prompt string, then
6	an LLM judge (openai/gpt-5.5 via OpenRouter) scores both target and reference on the task's
7	dynamic-weighted dimensions. Overall = target/(target+reference) (0.5 == parity with reference).
8	
9	This wrapper: (1) reads a report.md, (2) writes it as the target jsonl under the benchmark task's
10	EXACT prompt (so ref+criteria align), (3) invokes the official RACE harness limited to that task,
11	(4) prints results/race/<model>/race_result.txt.
12	
13	Usage:
14	    set -a && . ./.env && set +a
15	    python scripts/score_report_race.py --report outputs/live_compose/run2/report.md \
16	        --task-id 72 --model-name polaris_task72
17	"""
18	from __future__ import annotations
19	
20	import argparse
21	import json
22	import os
23	import subprocess
24	import sys
25	from pathlib import Path
26	
27	ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parents[1]
28	DRB = ROOT / "third_party" / "deep_research_bench"
29	
30	
31	def main() -> int:
32	    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser(description=__doc__)
33	    ap.add_argument("--report", required=True, help="path to report.md")
34	    ap.add_argument("--task-id", default="72")
35	    ap.add_argument("--model-name", default="polaris_task72")
36	    ap.add_argument("--race-model", default=os.getenv("RACE_MODEL", "openai/gpt-5.5"))
37	    ap.add_argument("--max-workers", type=int, default=4)
38	    args = ap.parse_args()
39	
40	    if not os.getenv("OPENROUTER_API_KEY"):
41	        print("BLOCKED: OPENROUTER_API_KEY not set — source .env first", file=sys.stderr)
42	        return 2
43	
44	    report_text = Path(args.report).read_text(encoding="utf-8")
45	    # Find the benchmark task's EXACT prompt (target/ref/criteria all key on it).
46	    task = None
47	    for line in (DRB / "data/prompt_data/query.jsonl").read_text().splitlines():
48	        o = json.loads(line)
49	        if str(o["id"]) == str(args.task_id):
50	            task = o
51	            break
52	    if task is None:
53	        print(f"BLOCKED: task id {args.task_id} not in query.jsonl", file=sys.stderr)
54	        return 2
55	    prompt = task["prompt"]
56	    lang = task.get("language", "en")
57	
58	    # Single-task query file so ONLY this task is scored.
59	    q_path = DRB / f"data/prompt_data/query_task{args.task_id}.jsonl"
60	    q_path.write_text(json.dumps(task, ensure_ascii=False) + "\n", encoding="utf-8")
61	
62	    # Target article jsonl (raw). Article carries the benchmark prompt so RACE aligns it with the
63	    # reference+criteria. This is an apples-to-apples score ONLY when the report was composed to
64	    # answer THIS prompt.
65	    target = {"id": task["id"], "prompt": prompt, "article": report_text}
66	    raw_dir = DRB / "data/test_data/raw_data"
67	    (raw_dir / f"{args.model_name}.jsonl").write_text(
68	        json.dumps(target, ensure_ascii=False) + "\n", encoding="utf-8")
69	    print(f"[race] target={args.model_name}.jsonl  article_chars={len(report_text)}  "
70	          f"prompt_task={args.task_id} lang={lang}")
71	
72	    out_dir = DRB / f"results/race/{args.model_name}"
73	    out_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
74	
75	    env = dict(os.environ)
76	    env["LLM_BACKEND"] = "openrouter"
77	    env["RACE_MODEL"] = args.race_model
78	    lang_flag = "--only_en" if lang == "en" else "--only_zh"
79	    cmd = [
80	        sys.executable, "-u", "deepresearch_bench_race.py", args.model_name,
81	        "--raw_data_dir", "data/test_data/raw_data",
82	        "--cleaned_data_dir", "data/test_data/cleaned_data",
83	        "--query_file", str(q_path.relative_to(DRB)),
84	        "--output_dir", str(out_dir.relative_to(DRB)),
85	        "--max_workers", str(args.max_workers),
86	        lang_flag, "--force",
87	    ]
88	    print("[race] running:", " ".join(cmd))
89	    r = subprocess.run(cmd, cwd=str(DRB), env=env)
90	    res_file = out_dir / "race_result.txt"
91	    if res_file.exists():
92	        print("\n===== RACE RESULT =====")
93	        print(res_file.read_text())
94	    else:
95	        print("[race] NO race_result.txt produced (see harness log above)", file=sys.stderr)
96	    return r.returncode
97	
98	
99	if __name__ == "__main__":
100	    raise SystemExit(main())
101

===== OUTLINE drb_72 =====
## Key Findings

_Each finding below is a verbatim, span-verified statement carried up from the body section named in bold; citations are the body's._

- **Foundational_Theory.** The framework centers on the concept that automation enables capital to replace labor in tasks it was previously engaged in.[1]
- **Empirical_Displacement.** ### Robots and jobs commuting-zone evidence (Acemoglu & Restrepo, JPE 2020)

Identification strategy: variation in exposure to robots—defined from industry-level advances in robotics and local industry employment.[3]

===== OUTLINE drb_75 =====
## Key Findings

_Each finding below is a verbatim, span-verified statement carried up from the body section named in bold; citations are the body's._

- **Efficacy.** Intervention: 40 infusions of a 500-mL chelation solution (3 g of disodium EDTA, 7 g of ascorbate, B vitamins, electrolytes, procaine, and heparin).[1]
- **Mechanism.** Exposure: iron status.[3]
- **Safety.** High‑dose zinc supplementation has been linked to nausea and an increased risk of infections.[6]
- **Dose Response.** A meta-analysis of 34 randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trials involving 2028 participants found that oral magnesium supplementation at a median dose of 368 mg/day for a median duration of 3 months significantly reduced systolic blood pressure (SBP) by 2.00 mmHg (95% CI 0.43–3.58) and diastolic blood pressure (DBP) by 1.78 mmHg (95% CI 0.73–2.82) compared with placebo, accompanied by a 0.05 mmol/L (95% CI 0.03–0.07) increase in serum magnesium.[2]

===== OUTLINE drb_76 =====
## Key Findings

_Each finding below is a verbatim, span-verified statement carried up from the body section named in bold; citations are the body's._

- **Efficacy.** Population: more than 1.7 million participants.[1]
- **Mechanism.** ### Pathogenic bacteria and their genotoxic metabolites in colorectal carcinogenesis

Pathogen: Escherichia coli.[3]

===== OUTLINE drb_78 =====
## Key Findings

_Each finding below is a verbatim, span-verified statement carried up from the body section named in bold; citations are the body's._

- **Efficacy.** Population: patients with advanced Parkinson's disease and severe motor symptoms.[1]
- **Mechanism.** Complication type: hardware complications.[2]
- **Long-term Outcomes.** The Movement Disorder Society-sponsored revision of the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale (MDS-UPDRS), validated in a study of 877 patients from 39 sites, demonstrated high internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha = 0.79–0.93 across parts) and strong correlation with the original UPDRS (ρ = 0.96), providing a comprehensive framework for tracking these changes across four parts: non-motor experiences of daily living, motor experiences of daily living, motor examination, and motor complications.[3]
- **Safety.** The FDA labeling for carbidopa-levodopa explicitly warns that patients should be observed carefully when the dosage is reduced abruptly or discontinued, especially if they are receiving neuroleptics.[6]

===== OUTLINE drb_90 =====

#!/bin/bash
# Compose gear-loop iter 5 — builds on iter 4 (section-level crash RESUME + self-relaunch). Two changes,
# both faithfulness-NEUTRAL (numeric forward-match + context-level NLI entailment UNCHANGED; the lexical
# >=2-word overlap gate stays DELETED at this branch head; nothing question-tuned):
#   (A) GEAR RULE (operator 2026-07-10): iter-4 HARDCODED the pinned cp3/cp4 paths. iter-5 RESOLVES the
#       NEWEST outline cp4 AND newest corpus cp3 at launch (ls -t) so the compose gear auto-picks up new
#       S3/S4 rounds as they land — the gears turn into each other. The pinned s4_gear/s3_gear ARE the
#       newest today, so pin + newest coincide; the dynamic resolve keeps that true on the NEXT S3/S4 round
#       without a launcher edit. Both resolved paths are PRINTED for the round report.
#   (B) 429 SELF-CONTENTION (observed in the iter-4 run 08:33-09:02: repeated OpenRouter 429 backoff +
#       a 3-min stall while a second heavy OpenRouter job ran in a sibling worktree). Drop the compose's
#       OpenRouter concurrency (PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM 30->16, writer 24->12) so the gear loop coexists with
#       sibling jobs on the shared OpenRouter rate limit with FEWER backoffs -> sections FINALIZE and reach
#       the iter-4 per-section checkpoint sooner (a kill then loses less work). max_tokens slate unchanged
#       (a CAP, not a target -> reasoning is never starved; §9.1.8).
set -a; . /workspace/POLARIS/.env 2>/dev/null; set +a
cd /workspace/compose_wt || exit 9
export PYTHONPATH=/workspace/compose_wt PYTHONIOENCODING=utf-8
# -- activation flags (ghost-free slate, iter-4 identical) --
export PG_COMPOSE_NO_RAW_SPAN_FALLBACK=1
export PG_SECTION_BASKET_MAP=1
export PG_SYNTH_PRIMARY=1
export PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER=1
export PG_VERIFIED_COMPOSE=1
export PG_VERIFIED_COMPOSE_MULTICITED=1
export PG_STRICT_VERIFY_ENTAILMENT=enforce
export PG_WRITER_DEADLINE_TRANSPORT_AWARE=1
export PG_CROSS_SECTION_REPETITION_GUARD=1
export PG_RENDER_SEAM_SANITIZE=1
# -- B4 resilience levers + throughput (iter-4 proven) --
export PG_WRITER_WALL_BASKET_SCALED=1
export PG_WRITER_KSPAN_RECOVERY_PASS=1
# iter-5 (B): lower concurrency to cut 429 self-contention with sibling OpenRouter jobs.
export PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY=12
export PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=16
export PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CALL_DEADLINE_S=300
export PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_WALL_DEADLINE_S=1400
export PG_ENTAILMENT_TOTAL_S=300
# iter-5 (Fable P0): the NLI verify pre-pass speedup (0615bc5) is capped at 4 by the process-global
# side-judge semaphore (judge_concurrency.py DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY=4, tuned for a credibility-burst
# 429 storm, not compose). 0 429s at 128-way proves rate limit is not the ceiling; raise the cap so
# verify threads don't queue behind 4 slots. Transport-only, faithfulness-NEUTRAL.
export PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY=16
# iter-5 (Fable P1): a hung judge POST holds a semaphore slot for the full PG_ENTAILMENT_TOTAL_S. The
# code comment (entailment_judge.py:196) prescribes the run slate set this to 1 (=> up to 2 attempts on

export PG_ENTAILMENT_TOTAL_DEADLINE_RETRIES=1

export PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_REASONING_MAX_TOKENS=65536
export PG_S5_SPAN_CHAR_CAP=8000
export PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS=2
